content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural grade reorganization (lower grades stay, upper grades centralize)'. Rural primary-school closures in demographically collapsing villages of under 2,000 residents risk accelerating population decline by severing community cohesion and family-settlement decisions, and are justified primarily by per-pupil cost differences that do not constitute a serious system-wide efficiency problem, making alternative models (satellite campuses, mixed-grade instruction, or controlled consolidation with local hub-school retention) preferable to outright closure.
opportunities: Enables full-time specialist subject teaching at the centralized upper-grade site, addressing the documented Polish problem of rural teachers commuting between multiple part-time postings.

Preserves a local school presence for the youngest, most travel-vulnerable children (grades 1-4/1-6), partially mitigating the documented burden of early-morning long-distance travel for young pupils.
strengths: Hungary's primary-school enrollment declined by 0.3% annually (2023/24 to 2024/25), representing a 17.3% cumulative fall over 20 years from 859,000 students in 2005/06 to 710,000 in 2024/25.
The number of school locations operating primary education fell from 3,550 in 2021/22 to 3,537 in 2024/25, a loss of 13 sites in a single year and 88 over three years.
threats: Poland's 1999 reform was politically reversible and was in fact reversed after 17 years; without concrete cross-party entrenchment mechanisms or constitutional-level protection, Hungary's version faces similar reversal risk, especially given the documented absence of any cross-party consensus mechanism for rural school reform.

Teacher redeployment under Act LII of 2023 (Status Law), which grants district centers authority to reassign teachers, may trigger the kind of mobilization seen in the Tanítanék movement if perceived as involuntary displacement without adequate consultation.
weaknesses: The causal direction between school closure and population decline remains incompletely resolved: does closure primarily follow depopulation (selection effect), or does it trigger secondary out-migration (treatment effect)? Finnish and Saxony studies suggest the latter with a 6+ year lag, but the mechanism and applicability to Hungarian administrative and cultural context are not definitively established.

The economic sustainability threshold—the minimum school size or village population below which per-pupil costs become budgetarily unmanageable—is not precisely defined in current Hungarian policy or research; this threshold varies by property condition, staffing flexibility, and central-subsidy design.
